analogy: Think of "Force and Pressure" through the chapter's own opening images: CHEMICAL EFFECTS OF ELECTRIC CURRENT CHEMICAL EFFECTS OF ELECTRIC CURRENT Your elders might have cautioned you against touching an electrical appliance with wet hands. But do you know why it is dangerous to touch an electrical appliance with wet hands? We have learnt earlier that... The easy idea is to follow the chapter's own clues one by one instead of memorising a loose definition. First notice the words and examples the text repeats: CHEMICAL EFFECTS OF, ELECTRIC CURRENT, 11.1 Do Liquids Conduct, CHEMICAL EFFECTS OF ELECTRIC CURRENT 139. Then connect each example to the question the chapter is trying to answer. Like solving a source-backed puzzle, every useful point must come from the chapter text itself and stay traceable to the NCERT source.
full_notes:
Force and Pressure is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins with its own framing, examples and questions, then builds the topic through source-backed facts. The notes below keep that order: first the opening problem, then the important terms, then the mechanisms, examples and exam-useful connections that appear in the chapter itself.

The text explains that cHEMICAL EFFECTS OF ELECTRIC CURRENT CHEMICAL EFFECTS OF ELECTRIC CURRENT Your elders might have cautioned you against touching an electrical appliance with wet hands.

The text explains that but do you know why it is dangerous to touch an electrical appliance with wet hands?

The text explains that we have learnt earlier that the materials, which allow electric current to pass through them, are good conductors of electricity.

The text explains that on the other hand, materials, which do not allow electric current to pass through them easily, are poor conductors of electricity.

The text explains that in Class VI, we made a tester to test whether a particular material allows the electric current to pass through it or not.

The text explains that do you recall how the tester helped us in deciding that?

The text explains that we found that metals such as copper and aluminium conduct electricity whereas materials such as rubber, plastic and wood do not conduct electricity.

The text explains that however, so far we have used our tester to test materials which were in solid state.

The text explains that paheli and Boojho want to remind you that one should not experiment with the electric supply from the mains or a generator or an inverter.

The text explains that use only electric cells for all the activities suggested here.

The text explains that to test whether a liquid allows electric current to pass through it or not, we can use the same tester (Fig.11.1).

The text explains that also, before using the tester we should check whether it is working or not.

The text explains that this completes the circuit of the tester and the bulb should glow.

The text explains that however, if the bulb does not glow, it means that the tester is not working.

The text explains that is it possible that the connections are loose?

The text explains that if they are, then replace the bulb with another bulb.

The text explains that due to the heating effect of current, the filament of the bulb gets heated to a high temperature and it starts glowing.

The text explains that the water that we get from sources such as taps, hand pumps, wells and ponds is not pure.

The text explains that small amounts of mineral salts are naturally present in it.

The text explains that (Instead of Small amounts of mineral salts present naturally in water are beneficial for human health.

The text explains that cHEMICAL EFFECTS OF ELECTRIC CURRENT 145 loss of copper from the solution is restored and the process continues.

The text explains that the process of depositing a layer of any desired metal on another material by means of electricity is called electroplating.

The text explains that the resulting effects are called chemical effects of currents. ‹ The process of depositing a layer of any desired metal on another material, by means of electricity, is called electroplating.

The text explains that (d) The process of depositing a layer of any desired metal on another material by means of electricity is called .

The text explains that it is found that the bulb of the tester glows brightly for liquid A while it glows very dimly for liquid B.

The text explains that the process that you saw in Activity 11.7 is used for purification of copper.

The text explains that (The process of electroplating in a commercial unit is much more complex than what we did in Activity 11.7).

The text explains that a cluster of white LEDs grouped together forms a LED light source.

The text explains that lED light sources consume less electricity and have longer lifetime than light bulbs and fluorescent tubes.

The text explains that hence these are gradually becoming the preferred lighting source.

The text explains that take a conducting metal plate and spread a moist paste of potassium iodide and starch.

The text explains that connect the plate to a battery as shown in Fig.

The text explains that now using the free end of the wire, write a few letters on the paste.

The text explains that cHEMICAL EFFECTS OF ELECTRIC CURRENT 149 Did You Know?

The text explains that lEDs (Light Emitting Diodes) are available in many colours such as red, green, yellow, blue, white and are increasingly being used for many applications, for example in traffic signal lights.

The text explains that lEDs are increasingly being used for lighting.

The text explains that for more information on this topic visit: l electronics.howstuffworks.com/led.htm 2024-25

Source: NCERT Class 8 Science, Chapter 11: Force and Pressure

revision_bullets: CHEMICAL EFFECTS OF ELECTRIC CURRENT CHEMICAL EFFECTS OF ELECTRIC CURRENT Your elders might have cautioned you against touching an electrical appliance with wet hands. (NCERT Class 8 Science Ch11 pg 1)
But do you know why it is dangerous to touch an electrical appliance with wet hands? (NCERT Class 8 Science Ch11 pg 1)
We have learnt earlier that the materials, which allow electric current to pass through them, are good conductors of electricity. (NCERT Class 8 Science Ch11 pg 1)
On the other hand, materials, which do not allow electric current to pass through them easily, are poor conductors of electricity. (NCERT Class 8 Science Ch11 pg 1)
In Class VI, we made a tester to test whether a particular material allows the electric current to pass through it or not. (NCERT Class 8 Science Ch11 pg 1)
Do you recall how the tester helped us in deciding that? (NCERT Class 8 Science Ch11 pg 1)
We found that metals such as copper and aluminium conduct electricity whereas materials such as rubber, plastic and wood do not conduct electricity. (NCERT Class 8 Science Ch11 pg 1)
However, so far we have used our tester to test materials which were in solid state. (NCERT Class 8 Science Ch11 pg 1)
Paheli and Boojho want to remind you that one should not experiment with the electric supply from the mains or a generator or an inverter. (NCERT Class 8 Science Ch11 pg 1)
Use only electric cells for all the activities suggested here. (NCERT Class 8 Science Ch11 pg 1)

prelims_traps: Do not treat CHEMICAL EFFECTS OF and ELECTRIC CURRENT as identical unless the source text links them directly.
Do not add outside examples to Force and Pressure; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.
mains_angles: Explain Force and Pressure through the source chain: evidence, process and visible result.
Use CHEMICAL EFFECTS OF and ELECTRIC CURRENT to show how the chapter connects facts with mechanisms.
Frame an answer around causes, internal working, surface expression and source limitations from the NCERT chapter.
